title: Battery Geopolitics: Balancing Industrial Power in the Race to Store Energy
gist: Carnegie's Milo McBride maps global battery-technology geopolitics, finding China already controls just over half of the 2025 global cell market and roughly 98% of LFP production capacity, with sodium-ion at even greater risk of near-total Chinese lock-in by 2030 (500+ GWh of announced Chinese capacity), while recommending OECD countries pursue selective Chinese joint ventures - especially for anode materials rather than cell factories - alongside targeted investment in silicon-anode and lithium-metal niches where real diversification is still achievable.

themes.theme: The strategic choice: compete, cede, or cooperate

insights.claim: McBride rejects both extremes on offer in the battery debate - ceding the sector entirely to China as economically efficient, or fighting an all-out onshoring battle - and instead argues OECD countries (the U.S., Europe, South Korea, Japan) should pursue selective cooperation with Chinese firms, especially joint ventures in anode materials, while building competitive niches in next-generation chemistries.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Sets the investment framing: bet on selective onshoring and joint-venture plays in specific chokepoints (anode materials, LFP niches), not a blanket 'de-China' battery investment thesis.
insights.quote: smart and tactful joint ventures in key technology verticals are a necessary part of this strategy under the right conditions
insights.locator: This does not mean cooperation with Chinese firms

insights.claim: McBride treats battery cell gigafactories - not upstream mining or downstream vehicle demand - as the key proxy for geopolitical battery power, because cell production is the most economically important value-added stage and its location determines where regional 'battery hubs' emerge.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Gives investors a single trackable metric - regional GWh of cell capacity under construction - to gauge relative shifts in battery power over time.
insights.quote: battery cell factories are used as a proxy for battery-related industrial development because they are the most economically important value-added part of the supply chain
insights.locator: Battery Tech and Market Trends

insights.claim: In 2025, two Chinese firms already controlled just over half of the global battery cell market, though three South Korean firms and one Japanese conglomerate still hold spots in the global top ten - a more diversified market than headline dominance figures suggest.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Current market share still has meaningful non-Chinese participation - the real risk window for total Chinese lock-in is the next five years, not today.
insights.quote: In 2025, two Chinese firms dominated just over half of the global battery market, but three Korean firms and one Japanese conglomerate still sit in the top ten.
insights.locator: Although Chinese corporations lead across battery technology

insights.claim: By 2030, Chinese battery cell capacity under construction could reach 5,862-6,720 GWh - roughly triple the combined 1,881-2,422 GWh of OECD capacity - and could exceed even the upper-bound global demand estimate of 5,100 GWh, implying a structurally oversupplied, China-dominated market.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Signals a coming global battery price war and margin compression driven by Chinese overcapacity - bearish for Western cell-maker unit economics regardless of subsidy support.
insights.quote: Chinese cell capacity could triple that of the OECD's members combined, hitting at least 5,862 GWh
insights.locator: By 2030, China's cell production will dominate

themes.theme: Trade flows and the cost gap

insights.claim: By 2025, Chinese battery exports totaled over $6 billion per month, nearly half destined for Europe, while North American imports of Chinese batteries were about $1 billion per month, driven almost entirely by the United States; only the U.S. and India have so far imposed tariffs or duties on Chinese batteries.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Europe is currently the most exposed, least-protected major market for Chinese battery imports - a policy gap likely to narrow as EU local-content and transparency rules tighten.
insights.locator: By 2025, monthly Chinese battery exports totaled over $6 billion

insights.claim: The Chinese cost advantage varies sharply by chemistry in the EU market: NMC cells see only a 10-27% cost gap versus Chinese imports, but LFP cells face a 24-50% gap; McBride estimates closing the LFP gap would require at least a 30% tariff or a U.S.-style $30/kWh production tax credit, or some combination of the two.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete policy price tag for any Western LFP protection scheme, useful for modeling subsidy-dependent Western cell-maker economics.
insights.quote: closing the cost difference would require at least a 30-percent tariff rate or a U.S.-style $30 per kilowatt-hour (kWh) production tax credit (PTC), or perhaps some combination of the two.
insights.locator: For LFP, closing the cost difference

insights.claim: New battery cell factories typically see 40-90% scrap rates during ramp-up, meaning factory throughput efficiency - not just headline capex - is a primary driver of unit economics; China is already deploying AI-powered process controls and digital twins to cut scrap and improve yields.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Western gigafactory investments face a hidden execution risk (scrap-driven cost overruns) beyond capex; AI-based process control is becoming a competitive necessity rather than an optional upgrade.
insights.quote: roughly 40 to 90 percent scrap rates from new battery cell operations
insights.locator: For newcomers to the market, this is a notable challenge

insights.claim: China is moving toward a fully automated 'dark factory' model for battery production, which will pressure Western competitors to automate as well, complicating the domestic political narrative that battery reshoring will replace lost internal-combustion-engine manufacturing jobs at similar scale.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Battery reshoring may create fewer jobs than politically promised as automation scales - a narrative and political risk for industrial-policy-dependent battery investments.
insights.quote: As China moves toward a “dark factory” model, wherein automated robotics are increasingly present throughout assembly lines, Western competitors will be inclined to reduce costs and improve quality through automation as well.
insights.locator: Critical political economy questions remain

insights.claim: 'Dry coating' manufacturing - replacing wet chemical binding with direct dry-powder application - can eliminate three of seven major production steps and cut manufacturing costs by up to 30%; a U.S. EV maker (identified elsewhere in the article's sourcing as Tesla, via its 4680 cell) claims commercial validation, with additional players in South Korea, Europe, and China also pursuing the process, though it is best suited to niche next-gen architectures like solid-state.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: A concrete, near-term cost-reduction lever for battery manufacturers to watch; adoption pace among gigafactory operators is a leading indicator of production-cost convergence with China.
insights.quote: could substantially lower manufacturing costs by up to 30 percent
insights.locator: The most notable example is “dry coating,”

insights.claim: China controls roughly 98% of global LFP (lithium-iron-phosphate) production capacity - a concentration that 'triumphs over' other strategic advanced industrial products like rare earth magnets, lithography equipment, humanoid robots, or AI chips, making LFP arguably the single most geographically concentrated critical technology in Chinese hands today.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: LFP is a bigger single-point-of-failure than chips or rare earths in relative terms - an underappreciated supply-chain risk for EV and grid-storage investors.
insights.quote: China dominates its geographic concentration at around 98 percent of global production capacity—a staggering market share that triumphs over other strategic advanced industrial products like rare earth magnets, lithography equipment, humanoid robots, or AI chips
insights.locator: Today, LFP accounts for about half

insights.claim: LFP battery technology was originally pioneered in North America before being sold off and commercialized at scale by Chinese firms, who took a chemistry initially dismissed as inferior (low energy density) and turned it into roughly half of today's global lithium-ion market - a cautionary precedent for how quickly a dismissed technology can become a Chinese-dominated chokepoint.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Illustrates the risk of Western firms divesting from technologies viewed as commercially marginal - a pattern worth watching in emerging chemistries like sodium-ion today.
insights.locator: The tech was originally pioneered in North America

insights.claim: Licensing or joint-venturing with Chinese LFP leaders to fast-track OECD production may be undermined by Chinese export-control policy, which appears designed to let Chinese firms build only lower-end factories abroad - China's fastest-charging, most cost-effective LFP technology is estimated to be about four years ahead of competitors - while keeping best-in-class production onshore, risking OECD lock-in to permanently second-tier technology.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Warns against a naive 'just partner with China' LFP strategy - technology-transfer terms need scrutiny before betting on China-licensed LFP joint ventures.
insights.quote: battery giants are to develop lower-end factories abroad while maintaining best-in-class production in mainland China.
insights.locator: Export restrictions from the Chinese government

themes.theme: Next-gen chemistries: sodium, silicon, lithium metal

insights.claim: Sodium-ion is almost entirely a Chinese commercial story: China has at least a dozen gigawatt-hour-scale sodium-ion facilities as of 2026, versus one South Korean pilot, zero in Japan, two in-progress U.S. projects (one of which, Natron, already filed for bankruptcy in 2025), and Europe's leading contender abandoning the technology.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Sodium-ion is the clearest repeat-of-LFP risk - the Western sodium-ion supply chain is essentially absent, which McBride flags as the paper's single most urgent policy priority.
insights.quote: one of its sodium hopes filed for bankruptcy in 2025
insights.locator: Sodium-ion is the most important story

insights.claim: By 2030 China could operate over 500 GWh of sodium-ion factory capacity - more than the current total lithium-ion capacity of the U.S. or Europe - and the CEO of China's largest battery manufacturer has publicly projected sodium-ion could capture 30-40% of the overall battery market and up to 50% of the affordable EV segment.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: If even partially realized, this reshapes affordable-EV competitiveness globally in China's favor within the decade - a direct threat to Western low-cost EV strategies.
insights.quote: the CEO of China's largest battery manufacturer expects that sodium-ion could corner between 30 and 40 percent of the existing battery market and up to 50 percent of the affordable EV market.
insights.locator: Beyond forecasts, the CEO of China's largest battery manufacturer

insights.claim: Sodium-ion's practical near-term advantage is not mobility range but stationary storage and cold-climate resilience - it avoids the thermal-control units lithium-ion requires, and sodium-based battery energy storage systems (BESS) are already being deployed commercially in both China and the United States.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Identifies stationary grid storage - not EVs - as the nearer-term commercial beachhead for sodium-ion, relevant to grid-storage and utility investment theses.
insights.locator: Furthermore and more importantly, sodium's energy density

insights.claim: Unlike sodium-ion, lithium metal battery technology shows genuine diversification: the United States actually leads China in lithium metal patents (27% versus 25%), though China still leads in projected 2030 gigafactory count (14 Chinese vs. 6 South Korean, 5 American, 4 each European and Japanese).
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Lithium metal is the one next-gen chemistry where U.S. IP leadership could still translate into a competitive domestic industry if scale-up execution keeps pace.
insights.quote: the United States leads China in lithium metal patents at 27 percent versus 25 percent
insights.locator: Remarkably, the United States leads China

insights.claim: Silicon-anode blends are the most geographically balanced next-gen chemistry: as of 2026 China leads with eight commercial-scale facilities, but South Korea is close behind with six, the U.S. has three, and Europe has a similar scale of projects, with strong 'drop-in' compatibility with existing lithium-ion manufacturing lines.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Silicon-anode is the most investable next-gen chemistry for Western and Korean firms today, combining technological readiness with less extreme Chinese concentration.
insights.locator: Much like sodium-ion, silicon anodes have fully entered

insights.claim: China holds about 43% of all 'high-integrity' battery patents filed in 2024 (patents filed in more than one jurisdiction, signaling real commercial intent), versus Europe's 21%, the U.S.'s 18%, South Korea's 10%, and Japan's just 2%; China's patent lead dates back to the 2000s, predating and outlasting the technology-transfer narrative commonly used to explain its rise.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Challenges the 'China only caught up via stolen or transferred IP' narrative common in Western policy debates - China's battery R&D lead is structural and long-standing, unlikely to be closed quickly by export-control policy alone.
insights.quote: China is responsible for about 43 percent of the total battery patents in 2024, while Europe holds 21 percent, the United States 18 percent, South Korea 10 percent, and Japan with a surprisingly small 2 percent
insights.locator: Today, however, China is responsible for about 43 percent

insights.claim: Lithium-ion battery pack prices fell from $827/kWh in 2013 to $108/kWh today and could fall another roughly 50% by 2030, while sodium-ion (currently as high as $124/kWh) could drop to $50/kWh with process innovation, and lithium-metal solid-state remains far pricier at about $157/kWh today, with Japanese firms targeting $75/kWh - still high relative to where lithium-ion is headed.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete cost curve for modeling when sodium-ion or solid-state could reach cost parity with incumbent lithium-ion - solid-state remains far from competitive.
insights.quote: the price of lithium-ion battery packs has been reduced from $827 per kWh to $108 per kWh and could be reduced by another 50 percent by 2030.
insights.locator: Since 2013, the price of lithium-ion battery packs

insights.claim: Wider adoption of next-gen chemistries, especially sodium-ion and silicon-anode, would structurally cut critical-mineral demand: a 2035 modeling scenario for Europe shows next-gen adoption cutting graphite demand by 25% and cobalt demand by 10% versus a scenario where these chemistries stay niche, even as lithium demand modestly rises.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Next-gen chemistry adoption is itself a mineral-security policy lever, not just a technology bet - relevant to critical-minerals investment and China-dependency risk models.
insights.quote: Graphite demand is cut by 25 percent and cobalt by 10 percent—arguably the two greatest geopolitical chokepoints of Europe's battery-related CRMs.
insights.locator: The results are staggering

insights.claim: Graphite, overwhelmingly produced in China, has become a key component of mineral weaponization by Beijing's Ministry of Commerce, making it arguably a more acute near-term chokepoint risk for Western battery and EV supply chains than lithium, which is more geographically diversified.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Graphite export-control risk deserves the same investor attention currently given to rare earths - it is a live weaponization vector, not a theoretical one.
insights.quote: graphite has emerged as a key component of mineral weaponization by Beijing's Ministry of Commerce.
insights.locator: Furthermore, graphite has emerged

insights.claim: Evidence of the pessimistic diversification scenario is already visible in the U.S. market: projected 2030 U.S. EV penetration has been downgraded from 46% to 24% amid policy reversals, with Western automakers writing off tens of billions of dollars in stranded EV investment.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Direct evidence the current U.S. policy environment is accelerating, not slowing, the ceding of battery/EV ground to China - bearish for near-term U.S. EV supply chain investment.
insights.quote: the projected U.S. EV penetration for 2030 has now been downgraded from 46 percent to 24 percent, with Western auto majors writing off tens of billions of dollars in stranded investment.
insights.locator: The U.S. environment for EVs has become unworkable

insights.claim: Some prominent American next-gen battery start-ups have begun relocating technology development to China to access idle Chinese factory capacity, risking one-way technology transfer, even as other U.S. battery firms instead choose joint ventures with South Korean conglomerates (such as POSCO Future M's investment in Factorial) to stay onshore.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: A bifurcation is emerging among U.S. battery start-ups - those partnering with Korea preserve Western IP position, those moving to China risk ceding it - a useful screen for battery-startup investment risk.
insights.locator: For example, some of America's prominent next-gen battery start-ups

insights.claim: Europe's battery ecosystem suffered a landmark setback with the collapse of Northvolt, its would-be domestic battery champion, while other Western battery scale-ups (such as Morrow) have also filed for bankruptcy, underscoring how few Western firms can match Asian competitors' gigawatt-hour manufacturing scale.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Repeated Western battery-startup failures argue against betting on stand-alone Western gigafactory bets in favor of joint ventures or smaller hub models.
insights.locator: Many of the innovative, small battery firms

insights.claim: McBride's central recommendation is to prioritize Chinese joint ventures for anode active materials - where China's 'unique corner' on the market makes technology absorption essential - over joint ventures for cell factories, while insisting on EU-style 'no sharing, no market access' conditions requiring genuine technology transfer and workforce retraining.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: Gives a specific, actionable sequencing for Western industrial policy - anode joint ventures first, cell-factory joint ventures a lower priority - useful for scoring proposed China battery deals.
insights.quote: Developing joint ventures with Chinese firms for anode production is a far greater priority than developing more cell factories with Chinese companies
insights.locator: Most importantly, efforts are urgently needed

insights.claim: Because Western battery start-ups can rarely reach Asian-scale gigafactories on their own, McBride recommends replacing the failed 40 GWh-mega-factory model with smaller ~5 GWh 'hubs' co-located with electro-active-material production and eventual recycling, anchored by cost-tolerant buyers like defense, aerospace, and telecom rather than price-sensitive EV demand.
insights.speaker: Milo McBride
insights.so_what: A concrete alternative investment thesis for Western battery manufacturing - smaller, defense-anchored clusters rather than EV-scale gigafactories may be the more survivable model.
insights.quote: reframe the goalpost of building a 40 GWh cell facility with, instead, building a 5 GWh hub with neighboring electro active material facilities and, eventually, co-located recycling.
insights.locator: Another option might be to reframe expectations
